title: 5 Practical Effects So Good They Still Don't Look Fake
dek: No CGI, no digital de-aging, no green screen — just puppets, prosthetics, and patience.

items.heading: An American Werewolf in London's transformation scene still holds up four decades later

images.caption: A still from An American Werewolf in London

items.text: The film's gradual, in-camera man-to-wolf transformation was achieved entirely with mechanical rigs and makeup, with no digital assistance. More than 40 years later, the sequence is still cited as a benchmark for practical creature work and continues to influence body-horror filmmakers.

items.heading: 2001: A Space Odyssey's effects predate CGI entirely — and still look convincing

images.caption: A still from 2001: A Space Odyssey

items.text: Stanley Kubrick achieved the film's spaceship and space-travel sequences using models, front-projection techniques, and in-camera trickery decades before computer graphics existed. Many viewers today still mistake shots from it for early CGI.

items.heading: Jabba the Hutt was a full-size, over-a-ton puppet

images.caption: A still from Return of the Jedi

items.text: One of the most recognizable characters in the Star Wars saga wasn't computer-generated at all — Jabba the Hutt was an enormous animatronic puppet reportedly weighing over a ton, operated by a team of puppeteers on the set of Return of the Jedi.

items.heading: The Thing's creature effects are still considered the genre's high-water mark
items.text: Rob Bottin's creature and transformation effects for John Carpenter's The Thing were built almost entirely practically, using animatronics, prosthetics, and puppetry. Decades on, the film is still regularly cited as the high point of practical horror effects.

items.heading: Mad Max: Fury Road leaned on real stunts over CGI — and won six technical Oscars for it

images.caption: A still from Mad Max: Fury Road

items.text: Director George Miller favored real vehicles, real stunt performers, and practical explosions over digital effects wherever possible. The commitment to old-school, in-camera action was widely praised on release and helped the film win six technical Academy Awards.
